mission.title: Install and Configure Opencost on Kubernetes
mission.description: OpenCost is an open-source cost monitoring tool for Kubernetes that provides visibility into current and historical Kubernetes and cloud spend. Installing OpenCost allows teams to gain insights into resource allocation and cost transparency across multiple applications and cloud providers.

steps.title: Add the OpenCost Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official OpenCost Helm chart repository to your Helm client with the following command:
```bash
helm repo add opencost https://opencost.github.io/opencost-helm-chart
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install OpenCost using Helm
steps.description:
Install OpenCost in your Kubernetes cluster using the following command. This will create the OpenCost resources in a new namespace called 'opencost':
```bash
helm install opencost opencost/opencost --namespace opencost --create-namespace --version v1.119.2
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the OpenCost pods are running successfully with the following command:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace opencost
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
To optimize resource usage, you can configure resource limits for the OpenCost deployment. Edit the values file or use the following command to set limits:
```bash
helm upgrade opencost opencost/opencost --namespace opencost --set resources.limits.cpu=500m --set resources.limits.memory=512Mi
```

steps.title: Access the OpenCost UI
steps.description:
To access the OpenCost UI, you can port-forward the service to your local machine:
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/opencost-ui --namespace opencost 8080:80
```
Then, open your browser and navigate to http://localhost:8080.

resolution.summary: A successful installation of OpenCost will show all OpenCost pods running in the 'opencost' namespace. You can access the OpenCost UI at http://localhost:8080 after port-forwarding the service.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add opencost https://opencost.github.io/opencost-helm-chart
helm repo update
helm install opencost opencost/opencost --namespace opencost --create-namespace --version v1.119.2
helm upgrade opencost opencost/opencost --namespace opencost --set resources.limits.cpu=500m --set resources.limits.memory=512Mi

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the OpenCost release from your Kubernetes cluster using the following command. This will remove all resources associated with the release, but not CRDs or persistent volumes:
```bash
helm uninstall opencost --namespace opencost
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Remove any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent resources that were created during the installation. Use the following commands:
```bash
kubectl delete crd opencosts.opencost.dev
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace opencost
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the OpenCost namespace and verify that it has been removed. Use the following commands:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace opencost
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, it's important to back up the current state of your OpenCost installation. You can do this by exporting the current configuration:
```bash
helm get values opencost --namespace opencost > opencost-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the OpenCost Helm repository and upgrade the OpenCost release to the latest version. Use the following commands:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade opencost opencost/opencost --namespace opencost --version v1.120.0
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
After upgrading, verify that the OpenCost pods are running successfully with the following command:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace opencost
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If you notice that OpenCost pods are stuck in a CrashLoopBackOff state, you can diagnose the issue by checking the logs of the pod:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace opencost
```
Look for any error messages that indicate what might be causing the crash.

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If you cannot access the OpenCost UI, ensure that the service is running and check the service details:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace opencost
kubectl describe svc opencost-ui --namespace opencost
```
Make sure the port-forward command is still active.

troubleshooting.title: Insufficient resources
troubleshooting.description:
If OpenCost is unable to start due to insufficient resources, check the resource requests and limits set for the deployment:
```bash
kubectl get deployment opencost --namespace opencost -o yaml
```
You may need to adjust the resource limits or allocate more resources to your cluster.

troubleshooting.title: Helm upgrade fails
troubleshooting.description:
If the Helm upgrade command fails, check the error message for details. You can also review the current Helm release status:
```bash
helm status opencost --namespace opencost
```
If necessary, you can roll back to the previous version using:
```bash
helm rollback opencost --namespace opencost
```

prerequisites.description: You need a Kubernetes cluster version 1.24 or higher and have Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
